executive_summary: The Swiss franc trades at USD/CHF 0.7787 and EUR/CHF 0.9165 as of May 7, 2026, near multi-decade highs on a real effective basis with the SNB Nominal Effective Exchange Rate at 130.0 — the cleanest stagflation hedge available in G10 with Swiss headline CPI at just 0.6% YoY versus Eurozone HICP 3.0%, US CPI 3.3%, and UK CPI 3.3%. CHF has appreciated significantly across all horizons against USD: USD/CHF down 1.3% over 1M, down 7.5% over 3M, down 11.2% over 6M, and down 13.8% over 1Y, with the franc the standout G10 outperformer alongside JPY. The dominant macro tension is the ongoing Iran-US war (Operation Epic Fury, begun February 28, 2026) — which simultaneously reinforces CHF safe-haven demand AND threatens an abrupt unwind on the active US-Iran ceasefire negotiations announced May 6. The SNB held its policy rate at 0.00% on March 19, 2026 with explicit language that willingness to intervene in the foreign exchange market has increased, creating a two-way policy risk: SNB intervention caps CHF strength, but US Treasury currency-manipulator scrutiny caps SNB intervention. Valuation is stretched: the Big Mac Index shows CHF at +48.4% overvalued vs USD (most extreme of 54 economies), real CHF at 25-year highs per BIS REER, but justified by the structural 7% current-account surplus, AAA sovereign credit, and Switzerland's status as the cheapest real-yield currency in G10. Our tactical stance is Mildly Bullish at low conviction 2/5 (down from March 11's 3/5 Bullish at +0.383): tactical net weighted score +0.105 reflects the offset between dominant Policy & Rates carry headwinds (-375bp vs USD, weight 0.24) and Inflation & Growth real-yield support, with asymmetric Iran ceasefire risk and crowded consensus long-CHF positioning capping near-term upside. We remain strategically Bullish CHF at conviction 3/5 (net +0.237) on structural pillars: 7% current-account surplus, AAA-rated 16.1% federal debt/GDP, the cheapest real-yield currency in G10, and the structural CHF appreciation trend that the SNB cannot decisively reverse without crossing US Treasury currency-manipulator thresholds.

valuation.what_is_priced_in: The market is pricing CHF as the structurally cheapest real-yield currency in G10 stagflation, and as the cleanest safe-haven complement to JPY in active geopolitical conflict. Valuation is stretched on every traditional metric but justified by the 7% current-account surplus, AAA fiscal, and uniquely low inflation that gives CHF a real-yield profile peers cannot match.
valuation.actionability: Valuation is structural, not tactical — CHF can stay overvalued for years (see 2011-2015, 2020-2022). Use as a medium-term governor on conviction sizing rather than a tactical signal; trim exposure on extreme overvaluation prints rather than fading the trend outright.

tactical.analysis: The SNB held its policy rate at 0.00% on March 19, 2026 for the third consecutive meeting, leaving Switzerland with the lowest policy rate in G10+SGD alongside the (now higher) BoJ at 0.75%. The SNB rate sits 375bp below the Fed (3.75%), 200bp below the ECB (2.00%), 375bp below the BoE (3.75%), 435bp below the RBA (4.35%), 425bp below Norges Bank (4.25%) and even 75bp below the BoJ (0.75%) — making CHF the funding currency of choice in carry trades and creating a structural headwind through the rate-differential channel. The Swiss Confederation 10Y at 0.35% is the lowest sovereign yield in G10; the 10Y spread to US Treasuries at -395bp is the deepest discount available outside JPY. This rate gap means each $10mn long CHF position vs USD costs ~$37,500/year in negative carry. However, this disadvantage is largely already discounted in spot — USD/CHF has fallen from 0.91 a year ago to 0.78 today despite this carry being known and stable. The tactical signal is straightforward: in non-crisis periods, CHF underperforms cross-rate carry alternatives, and with Iran ceasefire in active negotiation (May 6 announcement), the probability of returning to a normal carry regime over the next 2-4 weeks is materially elevated. The tactical weight on Policy & Rates (0.24) is the highest in the framework, and the signal here is unambiguously CHF-negative even if the strength of signal is muted by the ongoing geopolitical premium.

tactical.what_is_priced: The market prices the SNB on hold at 0% through end-2026 with a ~25-40% probability of negative rates only in extreme tail scenarios. Our view aligns: Schlegel's 'high bar' rhetoric on negative rates and the 0.6% CPI print (above the lower target band) make a cut unlikely without a sustained EUR/CHF break below 0.90. Where we differ is on relative pricing: the carry disadvantage is being underweighted vs positioning and safe-haven flows in current pricing — if Iran resolves, this factor reasserts dominance.

strategic.analysis: On a strategic horizon, the rate-differential headwind persists but compresses in absolute terms as global central banks ease. The Fed is OIS-priced for one cut by year-end (3.50%); the ECB likely holds at 2.00% with hawkish risks; the BoE could cut once to 3.50%; the BoJ is the standout hiker (priced 50% to 1.00% by Q3). Net effect over 6 months: CHF carry gap to USD compresses from -375bp to ~-325bp; gap to EUR essentially unchanged at -200bp; gap to JPY narrows to ~-25bp. The carry burden softens but does not reverse over the strategic horizon — CHF remains the lowest-yielding G10 currency. The KEY strategic pivot would be a sustained move BELOW EUR/CHF 0.90 forcing the SNB to cut to negative rates: at -0.25%, CHF carry gap to EUR widens to -225bp and CHF becomes structurally weaker. UBS, Goldman, and ING all anchor their strategic CHF forecasts on the SNB holding at 0% and intervening in size before resorting to negatives. Against the carry headwind, CHF benefits structurally from the 7% current-account surplus generating ~$70bn/yr of net structural inflows — equivalent to ~10% of CHF M2 — which mechanically supports the franc independent of carry. Net: the strategic Policy & Rates signal remains bearish but lower confidence than tactical because the carry compression and structural offsets temper the headwind.

carry_analysis.carry_regime: Carry-suppressed since the Iran war began. Carry strategies have lost money on long-USD/CHF positions for 12 of the last 14 months as safe-haven and positioning dynamics overwhelmed the differential. The regime has shifted from carry-dominant to safe-haven-dominant.
carry_analysis.breakeven_move: At -375bp annual carry vs USD, USD/CHF must fall ~290 pips per year for long-CHF to break even. Over 2-4 week horizons, this means CHF needs to gain only ~25 pips on USD/CHF to neutralise carry, which has been routine. At strategic horizons, the breakeven hurdle compounds: 6M holding period requires ~145 pips of CHF appreciation to neutralise carry — a higher bar.

tactical.analysis: Switzerland's April 2026 CPI accelerated to +0.6% YoY (the highest reading since December 2024) on energy-driven import-cost passthrough from the Iran-war oil shock. Critically, core CPI (excluding fresh and seasonal products, energy, fuel) eased to +0.3% YoY — the softest reading since July 2021 — confirming the inflation pickup is purely import-energy-driven, not domestic. This bifurcation is enormously CHF-bullish: while every other G10 economy faces import-energy AND domestic inflation pressure simultaneously, Switzerland has only the import component, and it is being heavily buffered by CHF strength itself (a self-reinforcing mechanism). The producer/import price index registered -2.7% YoY in March (sharpest annual deflation since November 2020), evidence that the strong franc is doing its disinflationary work. Q4 2025 GDP rose +0.2% q/q sport-adjusted, with full-year 2025 at +1.4%, and the SECO has revised 2026 GDP forecast modestly DOWN to 1.0% (from 1.1%) on Iran-war drag. Leading indicators are mixed-positive: the procure.ch/UBS Manufacturing PMI printed 54.5 in April (strongest since October 2022) but with the purchasing-prices sub-index spiking to 82.8 (up 11.5pts), and the KOF Economic Barometer rose to 97.9 in April from 95.6 in March (still below the 100 medium-term average). Unemployment held at 3.0% in April. The tactical inflation/growth signal is unambiguously CHF-positive: real Swiss yields are the highest in G10 by a wide margin (Swiss real 10Y -0.25%; vs Eurozone real 10Y -0.40%; vs US real 10Y +1.00% but with deteriorating data quality). The CHF real-yield premium trade is the cleanest expression of stagflation hedging in G10.

We agree directionally but think the market is UNDER-pricing the disinflationary feedback from CHF strength: if EUR/CHF stays below 0.93 for another quarter, the import-deflation channel intensifies, pulling headline CPI back below 0.5% by Q4 — making the SNB negative-rates conversation re-emerge. This is the cleanest CHF asymmetric-bullish signal on the dossier.

strategic.analysis: On a strategic horizon, Switzerland's structural growth and inflation profile remains uniquely supportive of CHF — moderate ~1% growth (well below 1.8% post-1981 average) but stable, with inflation anchored 0.3-0.7% range (compared to a 0-2% target band). The risk to the strategic Bullish stance is asymmetric: if Iran-war effects fade and CHF strengthens further, headline CPI could undershoot 0% by year-end forcing SNB to negative rates, which historically has been a delayed-but-decisive CHF-bearish trigger (2015 episode: -0.75% rate took 3 years to weaken CHF meaningfully; 2022 episode: rate normalisation was net CHF-positive). Conversely, if Iran resolves and oil falls back to $75-80, Swiss CPI eases mechanically and the negative-rates risk recedes. SECO's 2026 forecast at 1.0% is below trend but above-Eurozone (which is tracking ~1.0% with downside risk) and below-US (~2.0%). The structural inflation wedge — CHF priced for permanently lower inflation than peers — is the foundation of the multi-decade CHF appreciation trend. Over 1-6 months, the trend is intact: stagflation peers, Switzerland's import-deflation buffer, and SNB's high bar for negative rates collectively produce CHF-positive carry on a real-yield basis. Strategic confidence is lower than tactical (60 vs 70) because the 6M horizon allows for Iran ceasefire + SNB action sequence to play out, introducing two-way risk.

tactical.analysis: The risk environment is uniquely two-way for CHF over the next 2-4 weeks. The VIX at 17.13 (May 7, 2026) is contained at the 35th percentile of its 1-year range — markets are NOT pricing crisis-level fear despite ongoing Iran war. World Gold Council (April 2026): markets appear to be treating the Middle East crisis and Hormuz shutdown as transitory. This benign-vol environment is mildly CHF-negative because it suggests the safe-haven premium can compress further if Iran negotiations succeed. However, gold at $4,719/oz (May 7) — up >25% YTD — and Brent at $100.06 confirm geopolitical risk premium remains embedded across alternatives. CHF behaviour through this cycle has tracked: gold (+0.3 to +0.4 correlation), VIX (+0.3 to +0.4 correlation), Italian BTP-Bund spread (-0.2 to -0.3 inverse correlation). The active US-Iran ceasefire negotiations create a binary near-term outcome: success → risk-on, CHF safe-haven unwind, gold lower; failure/re-escalation → risk-off, CHF higher, gold higher. Both tails have non-trivial probability based on May 6 reporting. The tactical signal is genuinely Neutral: CHF's safe-haven character is primed but the catalyst is binary and unpredictable. SMI at 13,136 (April 30 close) reflects defensive Swiss equity rotation supporting CHF earnings translation. With both bull and bear cases credible at 35-40% each, Neutral is the analytically honest stance.

strategic.analysis: On a strategic horizon, the risk-environment backdrop favours CHF for three structural reasons. First, even if Iran resolves, the next 6 months feature multiple geopolitical flashpoints — US-China trade frictions, European fiscal stress, EM debt vulnerabilities, China property — any of which can re-activate safe-haven flows. Second, the global stagflation regime is structurally CHF-positive: in stagflation, traditional risk-on/risk-off dichotomies break down and CHF benefits as the cheapest real-yield currency rather than just as a safe haven. Third, CHF's beta to risk-off has DECLINED slightly versus history — the standard 'VIX up = CHF up' relationship is intact but at a lower coefficient than 2011-2015 — because SNB intervention rhetoric provides a partial damper. ING G10 Outlook 2026: real CHF at 25-year highs, validated by macro fundamentals. Net: CHF is mildly Bullish on strategic risk grounds but with moderate confidence (55) given the 6M horizon includes substantial path-dependency on Iran and macro data evolution.

The asymmetry is roughly: 30% chance of a +200 pip CHF squeeze vs 25% chance of a +150 pip CHF unwind — net asymmetrically Bullish CHF but only marginally.

strategic.analysis: On a strategic horizon, CFTC positioning is a less powerful signal because over 6 months, positioning normalises and unwinds become orderly rather than violent. The structural net-short is consistent with CHF's funding-currency status; what matters strategically is whether the regime shifts AWAY from carry-funding (which would reduce the structural short). The Iran war + global stagflation is a regime shift, but how durable? If by Q3 2026 oil settles below $80 and global growth stabilises, asset managers slowly resume CHF shorts and positioning normalises. If stagflation persists or deepens, the structural short becomes increasingly mismatched with reality and a slow-burn positioning normalisation supports CHF for several quarters. Net: positioning is mildly Bullish CHF strategically but with low confidence (50) because the signal is less crisp at this horizon.

strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price positioning normalisation gradually over 6M (slow short unwind). Our view: the normalisation may be slower than priced if stagflation persists, which would maintain the structural CHF squeeze potential.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both Bullish CHF; confidence higher tactical (55 vs 50) because the squeeze-asymmetry mechanism is more reliable at 2-4 week horizons than at 6M, where positioning normalises.
factors.contrarian_analysis_tactical: Positioning IS stretched on the asset-manager side — the -37.5k net short is in the top 5% of 5Y history (95th percentile). For CHF, stretched begins around -30k contracts (vs -50k+ for EUR or JPY where open interest is larger). Unwind triggers: (a) Iran re-escalation forcing immediate hedge demand → asset managers cover quickly; (b) SNB cuts to negative rates and announces large-scale intervention → asset managers re-engage shorts (the OPPOSITE of unwind); (c) sustained CHF appreciation breaking 0.7600 USD/CHF → systematic stops triggered → cascade short-covering. Squeeze severity: reference 2011 (CHF safe-haven peak) — net specs went from -40k to flat (unwind of 40k contracts) in 8 weeks, USD/CHF fell 12% during. Current squeeze potential: similar magnitude, ~10-15% USD/CHF move feasible if all triggers align. Recent precedent: March 2026 squeeze drove 150 pips in 48 hours. Other side of the trade: CHF shorts may be VINDICATED if (a) Iran resolves with full Hormuz reopening, oil falls to $70, safe-haven premium evaporates; (b) SNB introduces large-scale FX intervention coordinated with US Treasury (unlikely but possible); (c) global growth re-accelerates and carry trades resume — in this scenario, USD/CHF retraces to 0.85 and existing shorts profit. Tactical signal: Mildly Bullish CHF (squeeze potential intact but reduced ceasefire risk).
factors.contrarian_analysis_strategic: On a 6-month horizon, positioning normalisation typically operates through orderly unwinds rather than violent squeezes. The structural CHF net-short is consistent with funding-currency status, and the strategic question is whether the regime shifts AWAY from carry-funding. If stagflation persists, the structural short becomes mismatched with reality and a slow-burn unwind supports CHF for several quarters. If Iran resolves and global growth re-accelerates, asset managers maintain or extend shorts and positioning normalises in the OPPOSITE direction (more short). Squeeze severity is moderate at strategic horizons — historical 6M unwinds when positioning hits 95th percentile have averaged 15-20k contract reduction over the period, supporting ~5-7% currency appreciation. The contrarian case at strategic horizon: long-CHF consensus may itself become crowded (sell-side bullish forecasts at GS, UBS) by Q3 if CHF appreciation persists, creating a different positioning vulnerability. Strategic signal: Mildly Bullish CHF (slow unwind if stagflation persists).

factors.block: Factor 5: External Balance & Terms of Trade

tactical.analysis: Switzerland's Q4 2025 current-account surplus printed CHF 7.0bn (vs CHF 23.5bn in Q4 2024), with full-year 2025 at CHF 61.7bn — equivalent to ~7% of GDP. This is one of the largest current-account surpluses in the developed world (G10 only Norway exceeds it on an oil-adjusted basis). The surplus is structural, driven by: (a) goods trade surplus 2025 CHF 54.3bn; (b) services surplus dominated by financial services and commodity trading; (c) primary income surplus from large overseas investment portfolio. Pharma exports continued to dominate at CHF 152bn (~53% of total 2025 exports). February 2026 trade surplus was CHF 4.4bn — largest since August 2025. The structural surplus generates ~CHF 5-6bn of net CHF demand monthly, which is the cleanest mechanical CHF-positive flow available in any G10 currency. Iron-ore-reliant AUD has 35% commodity-export concentration; CHF has pharma-services concentration which is far less cyclical. Iran war marginally helps via gold trade flows: Switzerland refines 65-70% of global gold and gold prices at $4,700+ are driving ~25% above-trend gold trade volumes. Net tactical signal: structurally bullish, but at this 2-4 week horizon the flow is too slow to dominate spot — hence confidence moderated to 65.

tactical.what_is_priced: Markets correctly price the structural surplus as a slow-burn CHF-positive but UNDER-price the gold-trade impulse. With gold up 25% YTD and Swiss refining absorbing global volume, gold-related CHF flow has run +30-40% above trend in Q1 2026 — a meaningful incremental positive that doesn't appear in headline trade data because of gold's distortion adjustments.

strategic.analysis: On a strategic horizon, the External Balance factor carries 0.16 weight (second-highest behind Policy & Rates) and is the foundation of the long-CHF thesis. The 7% current-account surplus generates ~CHF 70bn/yr of net CHF demand — equivalent to 10% of CHF M2 — which mechanically supports the franc independent of carry, sentiment, or positioning. This structural flow is durable: pharma exports are non-cyclical, services exports diversified, primary income ~CHF 30bn/yr from accumulated overseas investments. Switzerland's structural surplus has been the main driver of CHF's multi-decade real appreciation trend (REER up ~30% over 25 years). The 6-month outlook intensifies this: stagflation environments favour Switzerland's pharma-services export mix vs commodity-and-cyclical exporters; gold flows are running well above trend on $4,700 prices; Switzerland's role as a financial services hub for war-related and sanctioned-asset flows is underestimated. Trading-partner concentration risk is moderate: 55% to EU means Eurozone weakness drags Swiss exports somewhat but pharma is relatively inelastic. China sensitivity is moderate (~7% of exports) — if China stimulates, marginal CHF positive. Strategic confidence is the highest in the dossier on this factor (75) because the mechanical flow effect is robust and well-documented.

strategic.what_is_priced: The structural surplus is well-known and partially priced. The mispricing is in the EXPECTATION that pharma exports will weaken on US tariff risk — the current US-Swiss bilateral trade dialogue includes pharma carve-outs given pharma's strategic importance. We expect pharma exports to maintain pace, supporting CHF flow longer than consensus.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both Bullish CHF; strategic confidence materially higher (75 vs 65) because the External Balance factor's mechanism (slow accumulating flow) operates much more powerfully at 6M than at 2-4 weeks.

tactical.analysis: The SNB's March 19, 2026 Monetary Policy Assessment introduced explicit FX-intervention escalation language: 'Given the conflict in the Middle East, the SNB's willingness to intervene in the foreign exchange market has increased. The SNB thereby counters a rapid and excessive appreciation of the Swiss franc, which would jeopardise price stability in Switzerland.' This is the most direct intervention signal the SNB has issued since the 2015 floor era, and it is unambiguously CHF-bearish at the tactical horizon. Chairman Schlegel reinforced this in his April 24, 2026 AGM speech: 'We have unrestricted room for manoeuvre with regard to the SNB policy rate and foreign exchange market interventions… In uncertain times such as these, we are particularly vigilant.' The SNB has 4 meetings/year (next June 18) and provides minimal forward guidance between meetings — but the recent Schlegel speech and the MPA escalation jointly create a clear hawkish-on-FX stance. The SNB's credibility is high (post-2015 episode notwithstanding) and markets do believe the intervention threat. The conditional inflation forecast was nudged HIGHER in the near term (energy-driven) but LOWER in the medium term (CHF-strength-driven), creating a deliberate signal: SNB is willing to accept higher near-term CPI to preserve lower medium-term CPI via FX. The tactical guidance signal is clear: SNB is positioned to intervene against further CHF strength, and this caps near-term USD/CHF downside.

tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price the SNB intervention threat partially: EUR/CHF stops have built around 0.92 (defending vs intervention) and 0.90 (assuming SNB intervenes hard). USD/CHF below 0.77 would likely trigger active intervention. We agree with the consensus framing but think the market UNDER-prices the constraint side: US Treasury currency-manipulator monitoring caps SNB intervention size at <5% of GDP. This means the SNB intervention threat is more bark than bite at scale.

strategic.analysis: On a 1-6 month horizon, the SNB guidance is two-way. If CHF continues to strengthen and inflation undershoots the lower target band, the SNB will be forced to escalate from rhetoric to action — first via large-scale intervention (CHF 30-50bn quarterly), then via negative rates (-0.25% potentially by September 2026 MPA). Once negative rates are reintroduced, CHF historically WEAKENS only with substantial lag (the 2015 episode took 2-3 years for full transmission). Conversely, if Iran resolves and CHF weakens to 0.80+ on USD/CHF, the SNB unwinds intervention rhetoric and the policy stance becomes neutral. The 6M strategic guidance signal averages across these scenarios: SNB is structurally hawkish on CHF strength but constrained in tools. The cumulative effect over 6M is probably mildly CHF-bearish if SNB acts (intervention + negative rates) and CHF-neutral if CHF weakens organically. Net: Neutral with moderate confidence.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical Bearish, Strategic Neutral. The divergence reflects mechanism: SNB intervention rhetoric is most powerful in the immediate 2-4 weeks (caps CHF strength); over 6 months, the rhetoric must transition to action, and the action set is constrained by external factors (US Treasury, EUR/CHF level), making the medium-term signal more balanced.

tactical.analysis: The dominant near-term event is the active US-Iran ceasefire negotiation. As of May 7, 2026, Operation Epic Fury negotiations have intensified via Pakistani mediators; Trump declared the operation could end if Iran agrees to terms, with the US naval blockade lifting and Hormuz reopening. Strait remains disrupted; oil swung 15% in 24 hours on May 6. The binary outcome is the dominant tactical risk: ceasefire success → CHF weaker via safe-haven unwind (USD/CHF could rally 3-5% within days); ceasefire failure → CHF stronger via re-escalation (EUR/CHF could break 0.90 within a week). Beyond Iran: UBS regulation parliamentary debate begins summer 2026; the Federal Council adopted dispatch on too-big-to-fail measures requiring UBS to hold ~USD 24bn additional CET1, a structural negative for UBS-related CHF flows but limited magnitude over 2-4 weeks. Swiss-EU Bilaterals III package signed March 2, 2026 in Brussels with Federal Council dispatch adopted March 13; parliamentary debate ongoing — long-term CHF positive (institutional anchor) but minimal 2-4 week impact. Net: tactical event risk is genuinely two-way and binary, hence Neutral with moderate confidence.

strategic.analysis: On a strategic horizon, even if Iran resolves cleanly, the next 6 months feature multiple low-probability-high-impact events that disproportionately favour CHF: US-China tariff escalations (Q3), Eurozone fiscal stress (Italian elections H2 2027 cycle starts), UK fiscal slippage, US debt ceiling redux (autumn). UBS regulation debate is ongoing but UBS retains strategic flexibility — net mildly negative for UBS but limited spillover to CHF. The cumulative tail-risk distribution over 6M favours CHF moderately; even a 30% probability of major risk-off event combined with CHF's safe-haven beta produces modest expected appreciation. Confidence moderate (55) because event risk over 6M is highly uncertain.

tactical.analysis: Switzerland's fiscal position is best-in-G10 by every metric. Sovereign rating: AAA across all four major agencies (S&P, Moody's, Fitch, DBRS) — all on Stable outlook with no rating actions in 2026. Federal debt-to-GDP: 16.1% (end-2025, ~CHF 140bn) — the lowest in G10 by a wide margin. General government gross debt: ~30.8% (vs Eurozone average ~85%, US ~125%, Japan ~265%). 2025 federal fiscal balance: +CHF 0.3bn surplus (the Federal Council confirmed at its February 18, 2026 meeting). Switzerland's debt brake fiscal rule constitutionally constrains structural deficits, anchoring the trajectory. Structural deficits projected 2027-2029 of CHF 2-4bn are flagged but small in absolute terms (<0.4% of GDP). The fiscal contrast with peers is stark: while the US runs ~6% deficit, UK ~5%, France ~5%, Italy ~4%, Switzerland is essentially balanced. This makes CHF the cleanest fiscal-quality bid in any global stagflation scenario where bond markets discriminate by quality. Tactical signal is strongly Bullish CHF with high confidence (80) — the fiscal advantage is structural and well-established but its IMPACT in spot is amplified during periods of fiscal stress elsewhere (which the current global stagflation regime amplifies).

tactical.what_is_priced: The fiscal advantage is well-known and broadly priced into the structural CHF appreciation trend. Where it is UNDER-priced is tactical: in a global fiscal-stress scenario (e.g., US debt-ceiling impasse this autumn), CHF would attract incremental safe-asset flows beyond what is currently in spot.

strategic.analysis: The strategic Fiscal & Sovereign signal is the most reliably Bullish CHF factor in the dossier. Over 6 months, peer fiscal trajectories deteriorate further: US fiscal deficit projected ~6.5% of GDP for FY2026 with $1.7tn supplemental defense spending on Iran war embedded; Eurozone fiscal slippage continues with German Schuldenbremse waiver under debate; UK fiscal credibility tested by Treasury reshuffle and gilt market pressure; France debt trajectory rising. Switzerland's fiscal trajectory is unchanged: balanced budgets, debt brake, AAA across agencies, no upcoming fiscal stress. The relative fiscal quality of CHF vs peers WIDENS over 6 months, providing a structural tailwind. This factor's strategic weight (0.07) is modest but its directional signal is high-confidence (80). The Italian fiscal trajectory is particularly telling: BTP-Bund 10Y spread at ~110bp may widen toward 150bp on autumn fiscal stress, and CHF historically captures the safe-asset rotation from periphery sovereigns. Swiss CDS levels remain inside 10-15bp range — among the tightest globally — versus US CDS at 35-50bp, France at 25-30bp, Italy at 60-80bp. This CDS quality wedge is a real-time signal of the structural fiscal advantage. The strategic risk to this factor is benign: only an unexpected Swiss banking crisis or major referendum outcome could meaningfully erode the AAA fiscal position over 6M, and neither is on the calendar.

factors.expression_assessment: IV percentile institutionally-reported elevated (~70th percentile 1Y) on EUR/CHF due to SNB binary risk; lower on USD/CHF (~50th percentile). RV vs IV: EUR/CHF RV ~6% vs IV ~7-8% means market pricing modest event premium → options modestly expensive. Risk reversals skewed toward CHF strength (CHF call bid) means directional CHF longs in options will pay up; spot expression more cost-efficient. Vol term structure flat to mild contango means no near-term event premium baked in. For directional CHF longs, prefer SPOT expression over options given (a) IV elevated, (b) RR skewed to CHF strength making options expensive, (c) no backwardation indicating cheap gamma. For SNB-binary expression specifically, short-dated EUR/CHF straddles into June 18 SNB meeting offer cheap gamma if buying straddle and the term structure remains in contango — this captures the binary intervention/cut/hold outcome efficiently. For positioning-squeeze expression, spot stop-loss reversals offer cleanest payoff. For consensus-divergence expression (long CHF/JPY), spot expression is cleanest given low-vol regime in the cross.

tactical.analysis: USD funding conditions are stable: Fed Funds at 3.75% upper bound, SOFR consistent with the band, no visible dollar funding stress despite Iran war. Cross-currency basis swap levels (3M EUR/USD, JPY/USD, CHF/USD) NOT FOUND with precision from public sources but institutional reports indicate basis has widened modestly during Iran-war risk-off but remains inside crisis thresholds — meaning USD funding premium has emerged but is not severe. Fed balance sheet stable around $6.6tn with iShares (Dec 2025) noting the Fed announced new T-bill/coupon purchases at ~$40bn/month for reserve management through April 2026. This is broadly USD-supportive (less liquidity withdrawn) but the magnitude is modest. The dollar smile framework places USD currently between right side (US exceptionalism, USD strong) and bottom (goldilocks, USD weak) — leaning toward the bottom as US data has softened and Fed cut expectations have built. CHF behaves similarly to JPY in this framework: strengthens at left side (crisis), weakens at bottom (goldilocks), weakens vs USD at right side. Currently we are at 'soft bottom' — CHF should be modestly weaker vs USD on dollar smile, but the safe-haven Iran premium has dominated. Tactical net: Bullish CHF as USD liquidity remains stable-to-easing, supporting CHF in the cross-current.

tactical.what_is_priced: Fed funds futures price 1 cut by year-end (3.50%); basis is at fair value reflecting modest USD demand. We agree directionally but think USD liquidity is biased EASIER over the next 2-4 weeks given Fed's bond-buying program continuation, supporting CHF (and other safe-haven currencies) modestly.

strategic.analysis: On a 1-6 month horizon, USD liquidity is biased structurally easier: Fed has limited room to maintain restrictive policy with US data softening; balance-sheet operations are now liquidity-additive rather than withdrawing. Cross-currency basis is likely to compress as funding conditions ease. The dollar smile dynamic over 6M favours CHF: as Fed eases and US growth converges with global, USD weakens on the smile and CHF strengthens vs USD. CHF as a funding currency benefits from easing USD liquidity in two ways: (a) lower USD borrowing costs reduce demand for USD funding via CHF unwind; (b) easier global liquidity supports CHF safe-haven positioning. Net: Bullish CHF strategically with confidence 60. The strategic Fed reaction function is the dominant variable: if US growth slows materially (NFP <100k sustained, ISM Manufacturing <48), Fed accelerates cuts and USD weakens broadly, supporting CHF. If US data resilient and Iran resolves, Fed holds and USD strengthens, weakening CHF. Cross-currency basis at 6M is likely to remain modestly USD-positive (small premium) but compress as US T-bill issuance peaks pass through summer and money-market absorption normalises. The strategic SNB-Fed standing swap line provides backstop liquidity that limits tail funding stress for CHF — an underrated structural positive. Reserve-currency dynamics over 6M are mildly CHF-positive: CHF share in global reserves at ~0.3% remains small but IMF COFER trend shows incremental rise as reserve managers diversify away from USD-EUR duopoly during stagflation regimes.

weighted_score.interpretation: Net weighted score of +0.1045 maps to a weak Bullish CHF stance. The score sits near the Neutral/Bullish boundary, reflecting the genuine offset between two strong but opposing forces: Inflation & Growth real-yield support (+0.126) plus accumulated lower-weighted CHF positives (External Balance +0.039, Fiscal +0.032, USD Liquidity +0.066, Flows +0.055, summing to +0.318 across the bullish factors) versus Policy & Rates carry headwind (-0.168) plus SNB Guidance (-0.046) totalling -0.214. The mild positive net is dominated by Real-Yield + Liquidity + Flow positives, but with binary Iran ceasefire risk creating substantial near-term tail uncertainty.

tactical.rationale: The tactical CHF case sits at a peculiar inflection: fundamentals (real-yield, structural surplus, fiscal quality) support the franc, but the dominant 'what's next' question is binary on the Iran ceasefire negotiations. Two factors carry the bullish weight at tactical horizons. First, Inflation & Growth at 0.18 weight delivers the cleanest signal: Switzerland is the only G10 economy with HEADLINE inflation rising on energy (0.6% YoY) but CORE inflation FALLING (0.3% YoY) — making CHF the cheapest real-yield currency to be long while every other developed-market currency faces stagflation cross-currents. Second, USD Liquidity (0.11 weight) is mildly easing as Fed bond purchases continue and the dollar smile transitions toward the bottom. Against these positives, the dominant tactical drag is Policy & Rates at 0.24 weight: CHF carries the largest negative carry in G10 by a wide margin (-375bp vs USD, -435bp vs AUD, -425bp vs NOK), and SNB Guidance (0.07 weight) explicitly targets capping CHF strength via FX intervention. The CHF rally has overshot historical safe-haven levels even controlling for the Iran shock — real CHF at 25-year highs per ING — leaving limited room for further upside without forcing the SNB into an aggressive intervention response. The factor interactions converge on this: bullish factors are durable but slow-burn; bearish factors are immediate and binary. The path of least resistance over 2-4 weeks is range-bound around current levels with EUR/CHF capped above 0.93 (SNB intervention rhetoric) and supported above 0.90 (intervention threshold), and USD/CHF range 0.7700-0.8000. Key tactical catalysts are the May 27 RBNZ meeting, the early-May Iran ceasefire developments, and June 4 ECB. Conviction is genuinely low — this is not a high-quality directional setup — and we recommend tactical SIZING discipline rather than added directional exposure. The single best risk-reward over 2-4 weeks is positioning for the binary Iran outcome via short-dated EUR/CHF straddles around the SNB June 18 meeting, capturing both ceasefire-rally and re-escalation-decline scenarios efficiently. Conviction drivers: the bifurcated April CPI print (headline up, core down) is the highest-conviction CHF-positive signal, but it is offset by the asymmetric Iran ceasefire risk and the 95th-percentile asset-manager net-short positioning that could be validated rather than squeezed if ceasefire holds.

g10_ranking.commentary: CHF is mid-pack tactically. Strong real-yield case offsets carry disadvantage to keep CHF competitive against most stagflation-hit peers, but loses to the higher-yielding hawkish-CB outliers (USD, AUD, NOK). Long-CHF crosses are best directed at lower-yielding peers (CHF/JPY, CHF/SGD, CHF/SEK) rather than against high-carry currencies. The ranking reflects CHF's structural advantages held back by the carry burden and SNB-intervention-cap on near-term upside.

strategic.rationale: The strategic CHF bull case rests on five overlapping structural pillars that compound over the 1-6 month horizon. First, the External Balance factor (0.16 weight) delivers ~CHF 70bn/yr of net structural CHF demand from the persistent ~7% current-account surplus — a mechanical flow that operates independent of carry, sentiment, or intervention. This is the single most reliable CHF-positive force on a strategic horizon. Second, Switzerland's Fiscal & Sovereign superiority (0.07 weight) widens against deteriorating peer fiscal positions — US 6.5% deficit, Eurozone 3.5%, UK 5%, while Switzerland balanced — delivering durable safe-asset bid. Third, USD Liquidity (0.12 weight) trends easier as the Fed shifts further into dovish territory and the dollar smile drifts toward the bottom, supporting CHF. Fourth, Inflation & Growth (0.10 weight) maintains CHF's status as the cheapest real-yield currency in G10 — a configuration that is structurally CHF-positive in stagflation regimes. Fifth, the structural appreciation trend itself is intact: the SNB cannot decisively reverse it without crossing US Treasury currency-manipulator thresholds. Against these, Policy & Rates (0.24 weight) remains the strongest individual headwind — CHF as the lowest-yielder by a wide margin — but the strategic carry gap to USD compresses 50bp over 6M as Fed cuts, and the gap to JPY narrows further as BoJ hikes. The strategic edge is in the cross-currency expression: long CHF/JPY captures the BoJ-hiking compression most cleanly; short EUR/CHF on rallies captures the SNB-anchored range trade efficiently; long CHF vs commodity-importer/cyclical pairs (CHF vs AUD, CHF vs NZD) captures the stagflation real-yield hedge. The path of least resistance over 6M is gradual CHF appreciation interrupted by SNB-induced consolidations — likely target USD/CHF 0.7500-0.7700, EUR/CHF 0.9000-0.9300. Key strategic catalysts: SNB June 18 (priced hold), September 25 (potential first negative-rate signal), December 11 (year-end policy reset); BoJ July (potential hike); Fed September (potential cut); US Q4 fiscal stress. Conviction 3/5 reflects the durable structural case offset by the substantial Iran ceasefire path-dependency. Actionability is highest at strategic horizon: position in size for the long-CHF thesis via cross-currency expressions, with tactical pullbacks as adding opportunities rather than reasons to fade.

CHF beats the USD strategically because US fiscal deterioration over 6M widens the relative-quality spread despite Fed staying above SNB on rates.

consensus_divergence.consensus_view: Sell-side aggregate forecasts (Goldman Sachs, UBS, ING, RBC, HSBC, Reuters survey) center on USD/CHF 0.78 and EUR/CHF 0.94 by year-end 2026, implying mild CHF weakness vs current spot. Goldman is the most CHF-bullish at 0.76/0.95; UBS more conservative at 0.78/0.94. The consensus core thesis: SNB intervention rhetoric caps further CHF strength, Iran resolution unwinds safe-haven premium, gradual normalisation toward fundamental fair value.
consensus_divergence.our_view: Tactically aligned with consensus on EUR/CHF range-bound but slightly more CHF-bullish strategically — we see USD/CHF at 0.76 and EUR/CHF at 0.91 as base case (more CHF-strong than consensus). The differentiator is in the structural pillars: real-yield premium, fiscal divergence, and positioning normalisation accumulate more CHF-positively over 6M than consensus implies.
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: 1-3% more CHF appreciation than consensus on a 6M horizon, primarily through stronger USD weakness channel and EUR/CHF compressing toward 0.91 rather than holding at 0.94
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: Four reasons. First, consensus under-prices the durability of the stagflation regime — most desks expect inflation moderation to push central banks toward easing, but Iran-driven energy passthrough is more persistent than priced; CHF's real-yield advantage widens accordingly. Second, consensus over-weights SNB intervention efficacy — US Treasury currency-manipulator scrutiny constrains intervention size, making the bark-vs-bite gap meaningful. Third, consensus under-prices the asset-manager positioning normalisation: structural net-shorts at top decile of 5Y history have to unwind to flat over time, providing slow-burn CHF support. Fourth, consensus under-weights Switzerland's fiscal divergence vs peers as a strategic tailwind that compounds over 6M as US, UK, and Eurozone fiscal positions deteriorate further.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: Strategic long-CHF position over 6M earns +2-4% outright; long CHF/JPY +5-7%; short EUR/CHF on rallies +2-3%. Combined relative-value book +3-5% with tighter risk profile.
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: Loss of 0.5-1% on outright long-CHF positions over 6M; flat on relative-value pairs. Risk is asymmetric in our favour given current spot — even if consensus is right, the loss is bounded by SNB intervention dynamics that cap CHF weakness.

cross_horizon_consistency.summary: Tactical (Mildly Bullish, +0.105, conviction 2/5) and Strategic (Bullish, +0.237, conviction 3/5) are aligned in direction with strategic conviction stronger. The divergence is in magnitude only — strategic horizons benefit from larger weights on External Balance (0.06 to 0.16) and Fiscal (0.04 to 0.07) which are CHF-positive structural factors, plus the transition from tactical Bearish Guidance (-0.046) to strategic Neutral (0).
cross_horizon_consistency.divergence_explanation: Tactical conviction 2/5 vs strategic 3/5 reflects different factor weight emphasis. At tactical horizons, Policy & Rates carry headwind (0.24 weight, -0.168 contribution) and SNB Guidance (0.07, -0.046) jointly impose -0.214 of negative drag against +0.318 of positive contributions, netting +0.105. At strategic horizons, External Balance (+0.120) and Fiscal (+0.056) increase with their higher weights, while Guidance turns Neutral (0 contribution), boosting net to +0.237.
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: Iran ceasefire outcome is the single largest near-term swing factor. If achieved cleanly within 4 weeks, tactical stance shifts to Neutral while strategic remains Bullish. SNB June 18 MPA outcome is the next inflection — hawkish hold reinforces tactical/strategic alignment; cut to negative rates introduces strategic uncertainty.

cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Express the view via strategic-horizon trades (CHF/JPY long, EUR/CHF short rallies) rather than tactical trades (which are diluted by binary Iran risk). Tactical conviction is too low to justify outright spot exposure beyond 0.5x normal sizing; strategic positions can be sized 1.0-1.5x with clear invalidation levels.

invalidators.mechanism: The combined policy response would historically erode CHF strength over 6-12M (referenced 2015 episode partial transmission). Negative rates plus large-scale intervention shifts the structural CHF-strong regime.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: SNB MPA decisions June 18 and September 25; SNB sight deposits and quarterly intervention disclosures

invalidators.condition: Swiss Q2 2026 GDP prints negative (q/q < 0%) AND KOF Barometer falls below 90
invalidators.mechanism: Swiss growth slowdown raises SNB's incentive to weaken CHF via easing. Both indicators undershooting simultaneously signals broader Swiss economic weakness that erodes the safe-asset bid.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: SECO GDP release September 3; KOF monthly

invalidators.condition: Swiss-EU Bilaterals III parliamentary debate turns hostile with explicit SVP referendum + breakdown signal
invalidators.mechanism: Removes long-term institutional anchor that supports CHF strategic case. SVP referendum risk crystallising would create political uncertainty discount on CHF.
invalidators.quantitative_threshold: Explicit SVP referendum filing + breakdown rhetoric

invalidators.condition: CFTC asset-manager net short CHF reduces to <-10,000 contracts (positioning normalises)
invalidators.mechanism: Removes the structural squeeze tailwind. With positioning normalised, the asymmetric upside from short-covering disappears and CHF must rely solely on fundamentals which face the carry headwind.
invalidators.quantitative_threshold: Asset-manager net short reduces to less than -10,000 contracts

signposts.threshold_confirms: Stays in 0.3-0.8% range (real-yield premium intact with structural inflation wedge maintained)
signposts.threshold_invalidates: >1.5% sustained two prints (eliminates real-yield advantage); or <0.0% sustained (forces SNB to negative)

signposts.threshold_invalidates: Closes above 0.95 for 5 consecutive days; or breaks below 0.88 (would force massive SNB response)

signposts.threshold_invalidates: Sustains <$80 (ceasefire success); or spikes >$130 sustained (extreme stress reduces broader CHF safe-haven uniqueness)

signposts.threshold_confirms: Stays net short -30k to -45k (squeeze potential intact)
signposts.threshold_invalidates: Rises to <-10k (positioning normalises, squeeze tailwind exhausted); or extends below -50k (oversold extreme, turn risk)

signposts.threshold_invalidates: Falls <90 (recession risk, SNB to ease aggressively); or surges >105 (reflation, SNB normalisation accelerates)

factors_changed.reason: At March 11, VIX was 23-24 with active Iran escalation; current VIX 17.13 with negotiation uncertainty creates two-way binary risk. Strategic remains Bullish.

Iran ceasefire negotiations active May 6, 2026 introduce binary near-term uncertainty that wasn't present at March 11 when Iran war was in active escalation. The 'buy CHF on Iran escalation' trade has matured.
CFTC asset-manager net short rebuilt to -37,534 contracts (95th percentile) since March 11; squeeze potential intact though reduced by ceasefire risk.
Trend has stalled into May consolidation; vol has compressed from 9% to 6% on EUR/CHF; signal weakened to range-bound consolidation.
Iran war effects making rate-differential channel reassert; SNB held at 0% on March 19 confirming no further easing while peers maintain higher carry.

signposts_triggered.outcome: EUR/CHF traded toward 0.90 in early March (low 0.9003 March 8) but did NOT break
signposts_triggered.implication: Validated SNB intervention threshold; spot has consolidated back to 0.9165. Threshold remains intact as critical strategic invalidation level.

signposts_triggered.signpost: SNB sight deposits — large-scale intervention threshold
signposts_triggered.outcome: No large-scale intervention announced through May 8
signposts_triggered.implication: SNB has remained in rhetoric-only mode; intervention bark has not yet transitioned to bite, leaving CHF strength room to develop further.

signposts_triggered.outcome: April CPI printed +0.6% YoY with core +0.3% (rising headline, falling core)
signposts_triggered.implication: Real-yield premium MAXIMISED via this bifurcation pattern; unique in G10. Confirms thesis with stronger conviction on Inflation & Growth factor.
New signpost crystallising; introduces binary near-term outcome that did not exist at March 11. Forces tactical conviction reduction.

conviction_change.explanation: Tactical conviction reduced from 3/5 to 2/5 reflecting the maturation of the Iran-driven safe-haven trade and the binary ceasefire risk that wasn't present at March 11. Tactical net score moved from +0.383 to +0.105, a meaningful weakening. Strategic conviction maintained at 3/5 because structural pillars (real-yield, current account, fiscal, USD liquidity) remain intact and the 6M horizon includes multiple catalysts that favour CHF cumulatively even if Iran resolves cleanly.

new_catalysts: Active US-Iran ceasefire negotiations (May 6, 2026 announcement) — wasn't on the March 11 catalyst calendar
RBA hike to 4.35% (May 5, 2026) — extended the carry gap to CHF
Norges Bank hike to 4.25% (May 6, 2026) — extended the carry gap to CHF
Schlegel SNB AGM speech April 24, 2026 — reinforced 'unrestricted' intervention rhetoric
Swiss-EU Bilaterals III signed March 2, 2026 with Federal Council dispatch March 13 — long-term institutional anchor

seasonality.pattern: CHF historically tends to weaken May-July (risk-on, summer carry, European holiday-season EUR strength) and strengthen August-October (year-end repatriation, autumn risk-off)
seasonality.relevance_window: May-July 2026 weakening seasonal partially active in tactical horizon (2-4 weeks); August-October strengthening seasonal active in strategic horizon (1-6 months)
seasonality.historical_magnitude: Average May-July CHF weakening: ~1-2% on EUR/CHF (since 2015); August-October CHF strengthening: ~2-3% on EUR/CHF
seasonality.current_alignment: The current regime — active Iran war, stagflation persistence — overrides typical seasonal patterns. The May-July weakening seasonal signal may be partially activated if Iran ceasefire materialises, providing additional downside risk for tactical CHF longs over the next 4-8 weeks. We assign a 10-15% additional weight to the downside scenario from seasonal effects. The August-October strengthening seasonal aligns with our strategic Bullish thesis and provides modest tailwind.

US Treasury currency-manipulator framework described qualitatively; precise threshold mechanics involve three criteria (>$15bn bilateral surplus, >2% C/A surplus, >2% of GDP intervention) with semi-annual review.
Big Mac Index publishers (Mappr, Statbase) cited for January 2026 vintage are aggregators of Economist data; primary-source verification recommended.
